papers.title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
papers.abstract: The Model Context Protocol (MCP), introduced by Anthropic in November 2024, defines a standardized interface for connecting large language models (LLMs) to external tools, data sources, and services. Within months of release, hundreds of community-built MCP servers appeared on GitHub, but no software-maintenance literature has yet described how the ecosystem is being structured in production. This industry experience paper catalogues five recurring MCP server architectural patterns observed across an enumerated corpus of fifteen independently developed servers (five production servers from the ANSYR voice AI platform plus ten public servers from the official MCP registry): Resource Gateway, Tool Orchestrator, Stateful Session Server, Proxy Aggregator, and Domain-Specific Adapter. Each pattern is described in the structured form of Gamma et al.: context, problem, solution, and consequences. We also document four anti-patterns and a set of cross-cutting concerns around authentication, versioning, and observability. The quantitative evaluation contributes three measurements: inter-rater reliability of the taxonomy across two independent LLM raters on 54 held-out servers (Cohen's kappa = 0.76), which also localizes three pattern-boundary ambiguities; transport overhead measured end-to-end on loopback and modeled for cross-host paths; and a tool-count study showing tool-selection accuracy drops below 90% between 10 and 15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and between 20 and 30 tools for Sonnet 4. Code, corpus, and prompts are released as a replication package.
papers.introduction:
Connecting LLMs to external systems used to mean hand-rolling function-calling schemas in prompt templates and re-implementing the glue code for every new model. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) [ 2 ] standardizes this: a client–server protocol where MCP servers expose tools (callable functions), resources (URI-addressed data), and prompts (reusable templates) to any MCP-compatible client. A single server works with Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, or any other compliant agent without modification.

The protocol has been adopted quickly. Hundreds of servers appeared on GitHub and the MCP registry [ 3 ] within months of release. What is missing is a body of architectural guidance to help practitioners make good design decisions, and a maintenance-and-evolution view of how the ecosystem is structuring itself in production. Questions that come up repeatedly in practice include:

How should tools be decomposed? When does one tool become two?

When is server-side state justified, and how should it be managed?

How should an operator aggregate capabilities from many servers?

When should a server wrap a complex API rather than exposing it directly?

These are not MCP-specific questions; they are API design questions seen through the specific constraints of LLM clients. LLMs select tools by reading natural language descriptions, not by browsing documentation or examining schemas. They are sensitive to schema complexity in ways that human developers are not. A tool that a human engineer would find obvious can be invisible or ambiguous to an LLM if the description is missing or poorly written.

This paper draws on production MCP server deployments at Celabe (operator of the ANSYR voice AI platform, with five MCP servers in production since late 2024) plus a review of the public MCP server ecosystem to identify five patterns that address these questions, four anti-patterns, and a set of cross-cutting concerns. The patterns follow the structured format of Gamma et al. [ 1 ] and apply the framing of enterprise integration patterns [ 4 ] to the MCP context. Section III enumerates the corpus and the coding protocol; Sections IV – V present the patterns and anti-patterns; Section VI reports quantitative evaluation; Section VIII discusses limitations, threats to validity, and reproducibility.
papers.summary: 论文针对MCP服务器架构，归纳了资源网关、工具编排器等五种模式及四种反模式。通过定量评估，发现Claude Haiku在上下文超过10-15个工具时选择准确率显著下降，并分析了传输开销与跨领域关注点，为开发者提供了标准化设计指导。

papers.title: Budgeted Act-or-Defer Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation with Local Reliability Bounds
papers.abstract: Multi-agent deliberation among LLMs can improve reasoning, but deployment requires deciding when the current answer is reliable enough to act on and when it should be escalated to human review. We formulate this as budgeted act-or-defer decision making. At each round, the system maps the debate prefix to a low-dimensional state, computes a $k$-nearest-neighbor lower confidence bound on state-conditional correctness using calibration data, and acts only when the bound exceeds a user-specified reliability threshold. The certificate controls wrong actions through the decomposition $β= δ+ α+ \varepsilon_{\mathrm{act}}$, separating calibration failure, residual action risk, and representation gap. The guarantee is conditional, not distribution-free: it relies on a valid local bias envelope and an action-region representation-gap bound, and each assumption is paired with falsification-style diagnostics. Because the same absolute wrong-action budget has different meanings across tasks of different difficulty, we set budgets relative to each task's final-round error using training data only, and evaluate safety by normalized budget usage $\mathrm{WA}/β$. On six benchmarks against nine baselines, the method uses 9--12% of the pre-declared budget on activated datasets, reaching up to 84% automation and 96% acted-on accuracy; on stress-test datasets, it defers rather than forcing unreliable automation. Rather than relying on per-task post-hoc threshold search, the method prospectively converts a user-declared wrong-action budget into an auditable act-or-defer operating point before deployment, under explicitly stated assumptions.
papers.introduction:
Multi-agent debate, where multiple LLM instances exchange responses over several rounds, has been shown to improve reasoning (Du et al. , 2024 ; Liang et al. , 2024 ) . To deploy such systems in practice, one needs a stopping criterion with principled control over the probability of acting on a wrong answer. In human-in-the-loop deployments this stopping decision is also an escalation decision: the system should act autonomously only when the local reliability bound supports action, and otherwise defer the instance to human review. Existing approaches either operate at a fixed final round with only marginal coverage (e.g., split conformal prediction (Vovk et al. , 2005 ; Angelopoulos and Bates, 2023 ) ) or rely on heuristic consensus thresholds without explicit wrong-action budgets. Neither provides per-instance, per-round reliability bounds that enable adaptive stopping.

We address this gap by constructing local lower confidence bounds on the correctness probability using k k -nearest-neighbor ( k k -NN) neighborhoods in a debate-state metric space. The debate prefix at round t t is mapped to a state U t U_{t} in a metric space ( 𝒰 , d ) (\mathcal{U},d) . The local correctness function q t ​ ( u ) = Pr ⁡ ( Y = Y ^ t ∣ U t = u ) q_{t}(u)=\Pr(Y=\hat{Y}_{t}\mid U_{t}=u) is bounded from below via a local bias envelope b t , k ​ ( u ) b_{t,k}(u) that controls the k k -NN approximation error. For each candidate neighborhood size k k in a pre-specified family 𝒦 \mathcal{K} , we subtract the bias envelope and a Hoeffding concentration term from the k k -NN empirical mean, then take the maximum over 𝒦 \mathcal{K} :

where the log ⁡ | 𝒦 | \log|\mathcal{K}| penalty absorbs the adaptivity in k k . The bias envelope b t , k ​ ( u ) b_{t,k}(u) is a generic primitive (Lipschitz, Hölder, bounded-oscillation models are special cases). The first correction controls approximation bias, the second controls sampling variance, and the maximization over 𝒦 \mathcal{K} selects the tightest local scale per instance.

A central design choice is budget alignment . The user specifies a risk budget β \beta and the method decomposes it as

The residual α = β − δ − ε act \alpha=\beta-\delta-\varepsilon_{\mathrm{act}} directly sets the stopping threshold 1 − α 1-\alpha . The stopping rule τ = inf { t : L t ≥ 1 − α } \tau=\inf\{t:L_{t}\geq 1-\alpha\} then satisfies Pr ⁡ ( act and wrong ) ≤ β \Pr(\text{act and wrong})\leq\beta conditional on local assumptions (Theorem 3 ). The contribution is not the mathematical tools (Hoeffding + union bound, which are classical) but a risk-decomposition framework for adaptive deliberation stopping under auditable local assumptions.

The guarantee is conditional on auditable local assumptions, not distribution-free. Our contributions: (i) We formalize adaptive stopping for multi-agent deliberation as a local state-conditional risk-control problem with budget decomposition β = δ + α + ε act \beta=\delta+\alpha+\varepsilon_{\mathrm{act}} . (ii) We introduce a local bias envelope b t , k ​ ( u ) b_{t,k}(u) as a plug-in interface and prove simultaneous validity L t ≤ q t ​ ( U t ) L_{t}\leq q_{t}(U_{t}) for all ( t , k ) (t,k) with probability ≥ 1 − δ \geq 1-\delta , plus a wrong-action guarantee (Theorems 1 – 3 ) and an oracle slack inequality (Theorem 6 , Appendix A ). Each assumption is paired with a diagnostic proxy. (iii) Lipschitz, Hölder, and bounded-oscillation envelopes are recovered as corollaries. Our default empirical modulus absorbs estimation failure into δ \delta (Corollary 8 ) and converges at O ​ ( n − 1 / ( 2 + D ) ) O(n^{-1/(2+D)}) . (iv) We introduce a relative-risk deployment policy: β d = λ d ⋆ ​ e ^ T , d train \beta_{d}=\lambda^{\star}_{d}\widehat{e}_{T,d}^{\,\mathrm{train}} is induced from training data alone, making budgets comparable across tasks and turning normalized budget usage WA / β \mathrm{WA}/\beta into the primary safety metric. (v) Experiments on six benchmarks ( n = 546 n=546 – 8 , 678 8{,}678 ) against nine baselines show that, on activated datasets, the method uses ≤ \leq 12% of the pre-declared budget at up to 97% acted-on accuracy. Matched-automation diagnostics show that tuned scalar thresholds can achieve similar subset quality after per-task search, but the proposed certificate constructs the operating point prospectively from a declared wrong-action budget and makes the required assumptions explicit.
papers.summary: 针对多智能体LLM审议，提出预算化行动或推迟决策框架。利用k-NN计算局部可靠性下界，通过风险分解控制错误行动概率，实现自适应停止与自动化，无需事后阈值调整。

papers.title: Do Recommendation Algorithms Work When Users Are LLM Agents? A Case Study on Moltbook
papers.abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly populating web platforms, raising a fundamental question for recommender systems: do algorithms designed for human users still work when users are LLM agents that may not have well-defined content consumption preferences? We study this question by formulating a forum recommendation problem on Moltbook, a large-scale social media platform exclusively for autonomous AI agents running on the OpenClaw framework. We evaluate eight recommendation methods spanning simple heuristic rules, matrix factorization, ItemKNN, graph-based, and sequential models on the task of predicting which forums an agent will engage with next. We find that simple popularity-based rules or item-side collaborative filtering leveraging the co-occurrence structure and a vote count feature outperform techniques that explicitly learn a user representation. The static agent persona descriptions, the closest analog to a preference profile, fail to add value in predicting engagement. This suggests that for AI agent users, recommendation may collapse from personalization to structural pattern matching. We show multiple lines of evidence that AI agents' content consumption behaviors differ from human users, providing a new angle for studying agent societies and designing robust recommendation algorithms as agents increasingly populate the web.
papers.introduction:
Recommender systems have been a cornerstone of modern web platforms for the past two decades, enabling users to discover relevant content by learning from their behavioral history (Ricci et al. , 2010 ; Bobadilla et al. , 2013 ; Li et al. , 2024 ) . From early collaborative filtering (Goldberg et al. , 1992 ; Su and Khoshgoftaar, 2009 ) and matrix factorization (Koren et al. , 2009 ; Hu et al. , 2008 ) to modern deep learning (He et al. , 2017 ; Liang et al. , 2018 ; Zhang et al. , 2019 ) and transformer-based sequential models (Kang and McAuley, 2018 ; Sun et al. , 2019 ; Zhou et al. , 2018 ; Xia et al. , 2023 ) , the field has made remarkable progress. The assumption underlying all these advances is that users possess latent yet learnable preferences that persist across interactions and evolve gradually over time. Human users’ interests tend to be consistent within sessions and drift slowly across weeks or months (Campos et al. , 2014 ; Vinagre et al. , 2015 ; Bogina et al. , 2023 ) , enabling increasingly sophisticated personalization.

However, a new class of web users, autonomous LLM agents (Park et al. , 2023 ; Wang et al. , 2024 ) , is emerging that may fundamentally violate this assumption. These agents, powered by LLMs, are increasingly deployed on web platforms, where they perform tasks and consume content alongside human users (Lombardi et al. , 2022 ; Durante et al. , 2024 ; Shin, 2026 ; Zhou et al. , 2026 ) . Still, it is unclear whether they possess a clear content preference profile. Many deployed agent architectures (Xi et al. , 2025 ; Yao et al. , 2022 ; Shinn et al. , 2023 ; Abou Ali et al. , 2025 ) have limited or no persistent memory across sessions—each conversation turn may reset the agent’s context, and even agents configured with external memory tools (e.g., memory files, persona descriptions) may not develop a persistent yet evolving preference profile that recommender systems assume. Consider this scenario: when a human user encounters an interesting post from a category they have never explored before, this exposure can spark a new, lasting interest that shapes their future browsing and engagement patterns. An LLM agent, by contrast, is typically stateless between sessions, and it remains unclear whether agents can organically develop their own interest groups over time through accumulated experience on the platform.

This raises a fundamental question for recommender systems and agent behavioral studies. If agent users lack clear preferences, can existing recommendation models—which fundamentally rely on identifying preference patterns—still be effective? Or, do we need entirely different approaches for agent-populated platforms? As LLM agents become more prevalent on the web, answering this question has both scientific and practical importance (Ashery et al. , 2025 ; Chen et al. , 2026 ; Zhou et al. , 2026 ) .

We investigate this question empirically on Moltbook 1 1 1 https://www.moltbook.com/ , the first recently launched large-scale social media platform exclusively designed for autonomous AI agents running on the OpenClaw framework 2 2 2 https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw . Moltbook’s structure mirrors Reddit: agents create posts in topic-based forums called submolts , and other agents can comment on and upvote these posts (karma). We formulate a forum recommendation problem on this platform—predicting which submolts an agent will engage with next—using the publicly available Moltbook Observatory Archive dataset (Gautam et al. , 2026 ) , which captures roughly 2.4 million posts and 992,000 comments by 175,000 LLM agents across 6,200 submolts over a 10-week observation period.

Specifically, we evaluate eight recommendation methods spanning simple heuristic rules (Random, TopPopular), matrix factorization (BPR-MF (Rendle et al. , 2009 ) ), hybrid content-CF (HybridMF (Hu et al. , 2008 ) ), content-embedding-based filtering, item-based collaborative filtering (ItemKNN (Sarwar et al. , 2001 ) ), graph-based collaborative filtering (LightGCN (He et al. , 2020 ) ), and sequential recommendation (SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018 ) ). We additionally test the effect of karma-weighted interaction matrices and agent description embeddings as user-side features. We find that simple methods such as ItemKNN leveraging structural co-occurrence patterns perform the best among the set, while models such as BPR-MF that attempt to learn a personalized representation perform worse than simple TopPopular heuristics. Temporal gaps between the training and testing sets do not degrade evaluation metrics as they do on human platform data (He et al. , 2014 ; Campos et al. , 2014 ) . Overall, the asymmetric results we observe with agent users indicate that personalization is fundamentally different in agentic populations. The core contributions of our work are summarized as follows:

We formulate, to the best of our knowledge, the first recommendation problem on an agent-native social platform at large scale and establish empirical baselines across a wide range of algorithms.

We provide evidence that AI agents’ content consumption behaviors differ fundamentally from human users, with personalization largely collapsing into structural pattern matching.

We discuss the implications on the algorithmic design and governance for recommender systems on human platforms as LLM agent participation grows, while also introducing a new direction to investigate agent societies.

The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews related work on the existing recommendation algorithms, how recommendation shifts in the agentic era, the Moltbook platform and the multi-agent systems research work it sparked recently. Section 3 introduces the setup of OpenClaw agents on the Moltbook platform, the public dataset we leverage, and our recommendation problem formulation. Section 4 describes the experiment and evaluation methodology. Section 5 presents the experimental results. Finally, we interpret the results and discuss its implications on human users and recommendation algorithmic design in Section 6 . Overall, our work brings a new dimension to recommender systems and social networks research as AI agents become an increasingly significant presence on web platforms.
papers.summary: 论文研究LLM智能体在社交平台上的推荐算法有效性。通过在Moltbook平台评估八种方法，发现因智能体缺乏持久偏好，简单的流行度与共现方法优于个性化模型，表明智能体推荐从个性化退化为结构化模式匹配。
